name: What to Do Immediately After a Utah Workplace Injury: A Step-by-Step Guide
description: What to Do Immediately After a Utah Workplace Injury: A Step-by-Step Guide - Boise Idaho

step.name: Step 1
step.text:
Getting injured at work is a disorienting experience. Between the physical pain and the fear of losing your paycheck, it is easy to make mistakes that the insurance company will later use to deny your claim. If you have been hurt on the job in Utah, follow these steps to protect your health and your legal rights.

### 1. Report the Injury Immediately
In Utah, you officially have 180 days to report a workplace accident, but you should never wait that long. Ideally, you should notify your supervisor in writing (email or text) before you even leave the premises. If your injury developed over time (like carpal tunnel), report it the moment a doctor suggests it is work-related. If you don't report the injury right away, the insurance carrier will argue that you actually got hurt at home over the weekend.

### 2. Seek Medical Attention
Your health is the priority. Go to an emergency room, urgent care, or your own doctor. **Critical Tip:** Tell the medical provider specifically that this happened at work. This ensures the records are coded correctly for workers' compensation. Be thorough—if your back hurts but your neck is also a little stiff, mention both. If it isn't in the first medical report, the insurance company will likely refuse to pay for it later.

### 3. Document the Scene and Witnesses
If you are physically able, take photos of the area where you were hurt. Was there a spill? A broken ladder? Lack of safety guards? Also, get the names and phone numbers of any coworkers who saw what happened. Employer 'incident reports' can sometimes be biased; having your own photos and witness list is vital for a fair claim.

### 4. File the Official Paperwork
Your employer is required to file a 'First Report of Injury' with the Utah Labor Commission. Ask for a copy of this report. You should also receive a claim number from the insurance company. If you don't hear anything within 14 days, your employer may not have filed the paperwork, and you may need to contact the Labor Commission yourself.

### 5. Follow Doctor's Orders Strictly
If the doctor gives you 'light duty' restrictions (e.g., no lifting over 10 pounds), you must follow them—even at home. Insurance companies sometimes hire private investigators to film injured workers doing yard work or grocery shopping. If you are caught exceeding your doctor's limits, your benefits will likely be terminated.

### 6. Know When to Call a Lawyer
Most simple claims go through without a hitch. However, if your employer denies the accident happened, if the insurance company refuses to pay for a surgery you need, or if you are being pressured to return to work while still in pain, it is time to seek professional legal advice. A lawyer can manage the deadlines and documentation while you focus on healing.
